summary: Research the design/psychology of fun in incremental RPGs → author docs/fun-factor.md → verify

research.practice:
I have enough across the touchstones. Synthesizing now.

---

# What works in great incrementals, and how to keep a 28.5h active-only RPG fun

Research synthesis with concrete patterns mapped to your 5-tier spine (T0 Estate → T1 Village → T2 Region → T3 Castle-town → T4 Edo). Every pattern is anchored to a real, well-regarded game.

## 1. Concrete patterns from the touchstones (moment-to-moment AND long-arc)

**Layers are new MECHANICS, not just bigger numbers (Antimatter Dimensions).** AD's Infinity / Eternity / Reality each introduce a new currency *and* a genuinely new system, which is why a pure number-go-up game holds a 89% "Very Positive" rating for "exceptional depth and replayability." Lesson for you: each tier transition must hand the player a new *verb*, not a bigger version of the old one. T0 = you swing the sword; T2 = you allocate retainers; T4 = you maneuver in Edo politics. If a tier is just "same loop, x10 numbers," it will feel like the slump.

**Hierarchical multiplier stacking (Cookie Clicker).** Buildings → upgrades-that-multiply-buildings → prestige-that-multiplies-everything → heavenly-upgrades-that-multiply-prestige. Each layer multiplies the one below. Your four pillars (Arms / Office / Estate & Wealth / Name & Honour) should be wired this way: deeds raise a pillar, pillars multiply House Influence, House Influence gates the next tier, and tier unlocks retroactively buff lower deeds. The player should *feel* the stack.

**Phase design with distinct end-states (Universal Paperclips).** UP works because early (production) / mid (markets + quantum) / late (space probes) are *different games* with different priorities and a real ending. Your 5 tiers should each have their own "win condition flavor," not one continuous bar. Frank Lantz's core insight: let players "hold exponential growth in their hands and feel the weight of it" — make the scale jumps legible and dramatic at each tier line.

**Always a visible chain of next things, none instant (Kittens Game).** Kittens is praised because the player "can see forward and recognize how one achievement unlocks others in a chain reaction," with ~200 techs and constant trade-offs. The design rule: at any moment the player should see 2–3 affordable goals, 2–3 visible-but-locked goals, and a silhouette of something far away. Never let the "next thing" be empty or instant.

**Interconnection makes every track matter (Melvor Idle).** Melvor's hook is that "all the hard work you put into one skill benefits others." Don't let your four pillars be silos. Wealth should fund Arms; Name & Honour should gate Office appointments; Office should unlock Estate yields. When one pillar plateaus, cross-dependencies pull the player into another — this is the structural cure for boredom.

**Momentum once systems interlock (NGU Idle).** NGU "starts goofy, then expands into energy/magic allocation, bosses, gear, challenges, wishes, cards" and gets its grip "once its systems begin connecting." The fun is combinatorial, not additive. Introduce systems that *modify other systems* (a crafting output that changes a combat option), not just parallel new bars.

**Variable-ratio reward inside a clear progress frame (grind psychology).** The satisfying grind = unpredictable reward (slot-machine dopamine) wrapped in *unambiguous* measurable progress. This directly validates your accrual split: the ~70% punchy deed-jumps are your variable-ratio hits; the ~30% per-season judged result is the "clear, measurable, anticipated" payoff. Keep both — they do different psychological jobs. Also: self-directed grind ("I *chose* to farm for that") beats mandated grind, so let players pick *which* pillar/deed to push.

## 2. Novelty cadence — never let the player plateau across 28.5h

The strongest pattern across A Dark Room, Kittens, AD, and Paperclips is a **steady drip of "first time you see X" moments.** Concrete cadence rules:

- **Reveal one new thing on a tightening-then-loosening rhythm.** Early (T0, 4.5h) reveals should come fast (every few minutes a panel/verb appears) to teach and hook; mid-tiers space them out but make each bigger. This is exactly progressive disclosure (Genshin-style): "introduce features progressively instead of dumping all controls at once."
- **Three reveal *types*, rotated so it never feels samey:** (a) new *verb/system* (a new pillar activity), (b) new *content within a system* (a new craftable, a new opponent), (c) new *modifier* (something that recontextualizes an existing loop). A Dark Room's genius is type-(a) reveals that "zoom out and reveal more of the world" — each one "shakes the player's preconceived notions."
- **Telegraph the next reveal.** Show a greyed/locked panel with a hint requirement. AD/Kittens both keep the player chasing visible locks. The anticipation is itself content.
- **Tie reveals to your season clock.** Use the per-season judged result as a natural cadence beat: every season can open one new affordance or shift what's judged. That gives you a predictable, ~28.5h-long metronome of freshness.

## 3. Escalating goals + the no-reset "fresh climb" (your hardest problem)

Prestige resets feel good (Clicker Heroes popularized "reset = power, not punishment") because they deliver three things at once: a fresh start, permanent power, and **a change in the unit of play** (what was hard becomes trivial). You have **no reset** — so you must reproduce those three effects *without* zeroing the player. The substitute, drawn straight from how Cookie Clicker / Universal Paperclips evolve:

**Delegation IS your prestige.** In Cookie Clicker/UP, "what felt laborious 30 minutes ago becomes completely automated." Your tier-up does the same thing *diegetically*: a rising samurai stops doing the chore and starts *commanding people who do it.* 
- T0: you personally perform deeds (swing sword, do the job, craft).
- T1: you direct a few retainers/household; old T0 deeds become a managed/auto-resolving track you *oversee*, not click.
- T2–T3: you govern groups/holdings; the unit of play is allocation and policy.
- T4: you maneuver at court; the unit is influence and intrigue.

Each tier promotes the previous loop "up the stack" — the player keeps all their numbers (no reset) but the *thing they actively touch* changes scale. That delivers the reset's "fresh climb" feeling because the moment-to-moment game is genuinely new, while the long-arc number never resets. This is the no-reset prestige and it maps perfectly to the Edo rank fantasy.

**Nested goal ladders (MMO grind structure).** Always run three goal horizons in parallel: micro (this deed), meso (this season's judged result / this rank), macro (this tier / House Influence threshold). "Small actions accumulate into visible transformation" only when all three are visible at once.

**Trimps' linear spine + perks gives the template for no-reset escalation within a tier.** Trimps marches through zones of 100 cells (a clean visible spine) while persistent Helium perks and equipment *tiers* make each stretch feel stronger. You can run the same inside each of your tiers: a visible "cell/zone" style march of ranks, with persistent pillar upgrades that make the next stretch hit harder.

## 4. The mid-game slump (your T2, the 16h tier, is the danger zone)

The literature is blunt: idle games front-load engagement and suffer a "content desert" mid-game that "drives the majority of churn" — "you just cycle and cycle, getting bigger numbers." Your 16h T2 is exactly where this kills you. Proven counters:

- **Force re-engagement with earlier systems (the "push old heroes to 1000" trick).** Add mid-tier mechanics that make the player *return to and re-optimize* T0/T1 loops with new tools, "making everything fresh again." E.g., a T2 reform that lets you re-run estate/village deeds at a new scale for a new resource.
- **Seasonal twists that change the optimal strategy (the "Pantheon Rework" pattern).** Use your season system to "change optimal strategy every cycle so returning players face genuinely fresh puzzles, not just buffed numbers." Rotate *what the seasonal judge rewards* (one season Arms-weighted, next Office-weighted), forcing rebalancing.
- **Introduce a brand-new system precisely at the slump's mouth.** Universal Paperclips drops the stock market / quantum computing right when base production would get stale. Schedule your most novel T2 system (e.g., a faction/intrigue or crafting-economy layer) at the *start* of T2, not the end, so the longest tier opens with its biggest "new verb."
- **Mid-tier interconnection payoff.** This is where Melvor/NGU interlock should "click" — the player realizes pillar synergies and the game becomes combinatorial. Design T2 as the tier where cross-pillar combos unlock.

## 5. Theme/narrative + UI-as-progression as fun *engines* (not decoration)

This is your biggest differentiator, and A Dark Room proves it can carry an entire game:

- **A Dark Room is the exact blueprint for "UI as progression."** It "withholds the object of the game" and "relies on the player's curiosity," and each new mechanic "zooms out to reveal more of the world." Its reveals *recontextualize everything before them* (fire-stoking → village → war → dying world). Your single-screen→multi-panel→multi-area reveal should do the same: each new panel shouldn't just add a button, it should reframe what the player thought the game was. The unlock of the "Office" tab should make the player re-understand their estate's place in a larger order.
- **Mechanical narrative > text narrative.** A Dark Room tells its story "through mechanical unfolding rather than direct narrative." Lean on this given your "open-ended, light-folklore" memory note: let the *structural* reveal (new tier, new map area, new political layer) carry the Edo decline-and-rise story, with prose kept sparse. The UI reveal *is* the plot beat.
- **Slow pacing between reveals lets players build a mental model** — A Dark Room's pacing "allows the player time to build up their own model of the world between reveals." For a 28.5h game this is a feature: your slow grind is the *space* in which each reveal lands harder. Don't rush reveals to fill silence; the anticipation is the engine.
- **Theme makes the numbers mean something.** Paperclips works because the theme (runaway optimization) gives the exponential a meaning you *feel*. Tie each pillar's numbers to fiction: Name & Honour rising should change how NPCs address you; Arms should change what you can survive. Numbers that change the *world description* read as story, not spreadsheet.

## 6. Active-only (no idle) — keeping minute-to-minute engaging

Without an idle layer, every minute must offer a *decision*, not a wait. Patterns:

- **"Meaningful decisions should be frequent, short- and mid-term goals abundant"** — this is the explicit active-incremental design rule. Your deed-jumps (~70%) are perfect for this: punchy, frequent, immediate feedback. Keep deed resolution *fast and juicy* (instant number pop, sound, bar fill — "visual metaphors for momentum").
- **Avoid dead waits.** Active-only means no "come back in 4 hours." If a deed has duration, fill it with a *choice* (which deed next, how to allocate, a risk/reward toggle), not an empty timer. The danger of active-only is that it becomes clicker-tedium; the cure is that each click should be a *small strategic choice* (Kittens' "play less, plan more").
- **Layer cognitive load over time so active play stays fresh.** Early = simple fast clicks (low load, high hook). Mid/late = the active task shifts to *allocation and planning* (Kittens/NGU), which is engaging for hours precisely because it's decisions, not reflexes. The delegation arc (§3) does double duty here: it keeps active play from becoming RSI by moving the player from "click the deed" to "decide the policy."
- **The seasonal judged result is your active-game anchor.** It gives every active session a near-term "exam" to prep for — a reason to optimize *this* session's deeds toward *this* season's judgment. That's the active-only substitute for the idle player's "check back later" hook: instead, "push to beat the season."

## Concrete pattern → 5-tier spine mapping

| Tier | New active *verb* (delegation arc) | Headline new system (novelty) | Anti-slump / cadence device | Source pattern |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **T0 Estate (4.5h)** | You *personally* do deeds (combat/skill/job/craft) | Fast UI reveals: one panel every few min; teach the deed→pillar→Influence stack | Rapid first-time-X drip; single-screen → first tabs | A Dark Room reveal; Cookie Clicker stack; progressive disclosure |
| **T1 Village (8h)** | You *direct* a small household; T0 deeds become an overseen track | First *interconnection*: pillars start funding/gating each other | Promote T0 loop "up the stack" (delegation = no-reset prestige) | Melvor interconnection; CC/UP automation-of-self |
| **T2 Region (16h) — slump zone** | You *govern* holdings; play = allocation/policy | Drop the biggest new verb at tier *start* (intrigue/faction or crafting economy); cross-pillar combos "click" | Seasonal twists rotate what's judged; "re-run early loops at new scale" reform | UP mid-game phase; NGU interlock; Pantheon-rework seasonal twist; "push heroes to 1000" |
| **T3 Castle-town (v1 stretch)** | You *maneuver* among peers/officials | New currency/layer that multiplies prior pillars (AD layer rule) | Telegraphed far-off Edo silhouette keeps macro goal alive | AD layered mechanics; Trimps perks/tiers |
| **T4 Edo** | You operate at *court* — influence & intrigue as the unit | Distinct end-state flavor (a "win" that reframes the whole climb) | Final reveal recontextualizes the saga (theme payoff) | UP complete-arc ending; A Dark Room final reframe |

**Cross-cutting rules for all tiers:** (1) Always show 2–3 affordable + 2–3 locked + 1 distant goal. (2) Keep deed feedback instant and juicy (variable-ratio ~70%) and the season as the anticipated fixed payoff (~30%). (3) Every UI reveal must *reframe*, not just *add*. (4) Wire pillars to interconnect so a plateau in one pulls the player into another. (5) The delegation arc (do → direct → govern → maneuver) is your no-reset prestige — it delivers the "fresh climb" by changing the *unit of play* while the numbers never reset.

---

result.authored:
Created `/Users/raynos/projects/games/incremental-game-1/docs/fun-factor.md` (tagged "DRAFT — for human review").

**Summary:** The doc establishes fun — the felt reward of voluntary effort — as the #1 priority for *Kamikakushi*, arguing that a mechanically-correct or story-coherent incremental that isn't fun is a failure, and that our two riskiest bets (the deliberate ~28.5 h grind and active-only/no-idle) are both *fun bets* that raise the bar rather than lower it. It names the seven core fun loops in our game (deed→reward, "one-more-rung", pillar number-go-up, the UI-reveal delight, the seasonal judged exam, combat tension + soft-setback, and warm narrative payoffs), each with why it's fun and the single lever that tunes it. It defines eight measurable fun *targets* (first-5-min hook, no-dead-time, reward cadence, always-a-visible-goal, novelty drip, the ≥30-min floor without a wall, the 70/30 feel, combat tension band) and explicitly hands measurement to qa-playtesting.md §3 while owning the "why these targets" itself. It gives six improvement levers in priority order (clarity → juice → pacing → goal/choice → novelty → rebalance-last), and a keep-it-high-over-28.5h plan built on the **delegation arc (do → direct → govern → maneuver) as our no-reset substitute for prestige**, plus the T2 anti-slump package. It closes with a fun-killers/guardrails table (each killer mapped to the specific PRD mechanism that guards it) and per-tier watch-points for T0–T2. Every claim is grounded in PRD §1.2/§1.6/§1.12/§1.13/§3/§4.2/§4.6.6/§4.8 and the two research briefs.

**Open questions for the human:**
1. The two key proxy thresholds are left as tunables — the **no-dead-time `~N s` ceiling** and the **reward-cadence `~X min` floor**. Do you want first-pass numbers locked now, or pinned during the M3 first-playable pass (my default)?
2. The **seasonal-twist rotation** (rotating *what the autumn/seasonal judge rewards* per cycle, Arms-weighted vs Office-weighted) is proposed here as a primary T2 anti-slump device — but it adds balance complexity on top of the already-locked 70/30 + high-water-mark seasonal model (§4.2.2). Confirm you want it pursued, or park it as "later."
3. The **T2 "re-run early loops at new scale" reform** ("push old heroes to 1000") is recommended as anti-slump — but it risks brushing against the "no city-builder/management-sim" guardrail (§1.13) if it grows a management layer. Want me to spec it as a strictly deed-based re-run (no new panels), or hold it for design review first?
4. One note flagged but not resolved: the FUN THEORY / PSYCHOLOGY research brief arrived essentially empty (only an `_id` line), so §2–§4's psychology grounding leans on the long-game brief plus established fun-psychology principles. If you have a fuller psychology brief, the loops/targets sections would benefit from a second pass.

verify.summary:
docs/fun-factor.md genuinely delivers the WHAT/WHY of fun and passes all four verification criteria; the only fixes are minor cross-reference cleanups (none blocking).

(1) WHAT/WHY, not tooling-rehash — CONFIRMED. §1 defines fun ("the felt reward of voluntary effort", L21) and argues why it is the make-or-break #1 priority (L30-48, including the one-line test). §2 is the fun loops, §4 improve, §5 keep-high. It does NOT duplicate the harness internals (window.__qa, the bot, the screenshot loop); it explicitly delegates measurement: "This doc sets the targets; plans/qa-playtesting.md §3 is the harness that measures them" (L7-8) and "this doc owns the intent... the other owns the realisation" (L9-11). §3 repeats the boundary (L146-149). The separation is reciprocal — qa-playtesting.md §3 itself points back: "What fun is, why these targets matter, and how to improve/keep fun live in fun-factor.md" (qa L111-112). The §6 "number/reward juice" and §3 "novelty drip" citations map to real qa categories (qa §6 "Number/reward juice (feel)", qa §3 "new-thing reveal... never dumped").

(2) Grounded in OUR game — CONFIRMED. 5-tier estate spine via the delegation arc do→direct→govern→maneuver→court (§5a, L208-215) and per-tier §7. Four pillars Arms/Estate&Wealth/Office/Name&Honour with interconnection + gate drift (§2.3, L82-91) — drift "Arms+Estate → Office+Name" matches PRD §1.6.3 (L409, L1452) exactly. UI-as-progression is §2.4 "the signature". 28.5h / 4.5+8+16 matches PRD §4.8 (L2922, L3035). active-only (D-013, L778) and no-reset (D-004 reversed, L770) both load-bearing. Specific named content throughout (Tama legend, Tomita/Akagi rivals, G7 climax, Jūbei/wolf first fight, meibutsu silk) — no generic platitudes.

(3) All 7 topics — CONFIRMED: definition/priority (§1), core loops (§2, seven), measure (§3), improve (§4), keep-high-over-28.5h (§5), fun-killers+guardrails (§6), per-tier watch-points (§7).

(4) PRD-consistent + actionable — CONFIRMED. Every quantitative claim verified against PRD: 70/30 split (§4.2 L2357), 0.04 per-event cap (§4.2.1 L2377), trade ≤⅓ (§4.2.3 L381), ≥30-min floor LOCKED (L2261), ≤2-3× never-balloon + 1.15× intra-tier (L2268/2270), soft-setback shape 1HP/~½-day/light injury/never levels-gear-Influence LOCKED (§4.6.6 L2816/2823), first-fight 20-35%→~85% (L2814/2823), recognised deed every ~4.5-5min (§4.8.1 L2966), first-action <5s (L2919), auto-producers T3+ (L832), never-a-wipe (§1.13 L740). Fully consistent with PRD §3 reveal cadence and PHF §K3 fun-process (proxies named identically: dead-time, reward/unlock cadence, always-a-visible-next-goal, first-5-min hook; human is final fun judge). Actionable: §4 order-of-operations (clarity→juice→pacing→goal/choice→novelty→rebalance, L193-194), §5d concrete T2 anti-slump package, §7 per-tier watch lists.

findings.issue: References ui-design.md as "(forthcoming)" / "the UI design bible (ui-design.md, forthcoming)", but docs/ui-design.md already exists and is a substantive 39KB authored bible (header: "UI Design-Language Bible — the Kurosawa house"). The cross-reference is stale — it tells the reader a doc has not been written when it has.
findings.fix: Drop "(forthcoming)" in both places and make it a live link to docs/ui-design.md (e.g. "the UI design bible ([ui-design.md](ui-design.md))").

findings.issue: Attributes verification of the 70/30 accrual split to qa-playtesting.md §2 ("the gates are reachable and the 70/30 split holds (qa-playtesting.md §2)"). qa §2 is the headless auto-player/balance engine; the 70/30 proxy is actually asserted in qa §3 under "Pillar/seasonal pacing: the 70/30 split holds". The bot (§2) produces the data §3 asserts, so it is defensible, but the precise proxy citation is §3 — and the same sentence already references §3 elsewhere, so the split citation reads slightly off.
findings.fix: Cite qa-playtesting.md §3 for the 70/30 split (or "§2/§3"), since §3 is where that proxy is defined; keep §2 for "gates reachable" if desired.

findings.issue: Names the third pillar "Office" where the PRD's canonical four-pillar table (§1.6, PRD L378/L1436) labels it "Standing & Office (官威 kan'i)". The PRD does use "Office" as shorthand in the gate-drift text (§1.6.3 "Office + Name"), so this is consistent with canon, but a reader cross-referencing the canonical pillar list may momentarily mismatch the name.
findings.fix: On first mention (§2.3) gloss it once, e.g. "Office (Standing & Office)", to bind the shorthand to the canonical pillar name; no further change needed.
